EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief period of warm conditions accompanied strong to gusty southwesterly winds across much of North-central and Southwest Montana. An Arctic cold front arrived shortly after, which brought much colder temperatures and accumulating snow to much of the Treasure State, including snow squalls to portions of Southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall. At 1:20 PM, Bozeman Airport ASOS reported light snow and 40 mph wind gusts with visibility down to 1.25 miles. 4 minutes later, ASOS reported wind gusts up to 44 mph and visibility down to 0.25 miles. These conditions continued for 10 minutes before visibility improved to 0.5 miles, but gusty winds between 30 mph and 45 mph continued for 1 hour. ASOS reported heavy snowfall for 5 minutes, then moderate snow for 10 minutes, then light snow for 20 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief period of warm conditions accompanied strong to gusty southwesterly winds across much of North-central and Southwest Montana. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall. Snow and blowing snow reducing visibility to a quarter mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief period of warm conditions accompanied strong to gusty southwesterly winds across much of North-central and Southwest Montana. An Arctic cold front arrived shortly after, which brought much colder temperatures and accumulating snow to much of the Treasure State, including snow squalls to portions of Southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall. Light snow started at the airport at 11:30 AM. Visibility quickly reduced down to 0.75 miles in 9 minutes. ASOS reported winds gusting between 35 and 39 mph. Moderate snow fall by the ASOS was reported between 11:45 and 11:55 AM with visibility down to 0.5 miles. Visibility improved to over 1 mile after 11:55 AM. Airport staff called at 11:45 AM with 0.8 inches of new snow at the airport from the snow squall that passed through and near zero visibility. FAA Webcam 15 minute imagery showed the reduction in visibility of less than a quarter of a mile.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall. Starting at 11:15 AM, the precipitation sensor was detected and live webcams showed precipitation increasing from the west. Road surface temperatures were recorded to be between 25 degrees at the start and 23 degrees by 12:30 PM. Wind gusts were between 10 mph and 23 mph from 11:15 AM to 12:30 PM. MDT 15 minute webcam imagery showed near zero visibility between 12 PM and 1 PM. Precipitation sensor stopped reporting activity at 12:45 PM and webcam imagery showed visibility improvement after 1 PM MST.

A mesonet site in Turah measured a gust of 54 mph.  There were reports of trees across the roads in Missoula.  The power company showed a few thousand without power.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media reported lots of damage in Seeley Lake.  Essential travel only was declared at about 5:17pm MST and lifted on Aug 24 at 5:46pm MST.  Social media produced several images of trees down on houses and decks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late August wildfires are documented here.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first report of the Marsh Creek Fire was made on August 27th late afternoon/early evening. The fire was 12 miles SE of Lincoln and was a lightning start. The fire grew to 151 acres before becoming 100% contained on or around September 17. Fuels involved were dense timber, live and beetle killed trees, and dead and downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wildfires that started in mid-late August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ratio Mountain Fire began on August 18 and was the result of a lightning strike 15 miles NW of Whitehall. The fire has grown to 1,966 acres and is not fully contained as of writing this in late September, 2024. Fuels noted were a large concentration of standing dead trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late August wildfires are documented here.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Hollow Fire 36 miles SW of Dillon began August 23rd, with cause undetermined at this time. The fire grew to 3,449 acres before becoming 100% contained as of September 9, 2024. Fuels involved include tall grass, timber, and brush.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Shortwaves traversing a broader longwave trough across much of the western CONUS brought a round of snow to the region. Most impacts came in/near mountains and across the plains generally west of I-15. Isentropic ascent combined with northwesterly surface winds to result in heavy and blowing snow, especially along the Northern Rocky Mountain front, where short-term (Less than 3 hrs) of whiteout conditions were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snotel Equipment at Nevada Ridge estimated 10 inches new snow for the event.

Intense snow showers were observed on the evening of the 15th,  with sudden reduced visibility leading to difficult travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwesterly flow on the order of 50-60kts at 700 mb was mixed to the surface across much of Southwest Montana into portions of North-central Montana on the 29th and into the 1st of March. Additionally, a Pacific cold front ended up mixing strong winds gusts to the surface on the afternoon of the 29th across portions of the plains where strong synoptic wind gusts were not forecast. Only one gust of high wind criteria was measured on March 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station MTBWG Browning measured two gusts in excess of 58 mph, including a peak gust of 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Shortwaves traversing a broader longwave trough across much of the western CONUS brought a round of snow to the region. Most impacts came in/near mountains and across the plains generally west of I-15. Isentropic ascent combined with northwesterly surface winds to result in heavy and blowing snow, especially along the Northern Rocky Mountain front, where short-term (Less than 3 hrs) of whiteout conditions were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOTEL equipment at Wood Creek estimated 10 inches of new snow over the duration of the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and moist air from the southwest lifted over shallow cold air to produce areas of moderate to heavy snow late on the 14th through late on the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow totals ranged from 6 to 8 inches in Hamilton with 8-12 inches on the benches around Hamilton and in the southern end of the Bitterroot Valley. Lesser amounts of 1 to 3 inches occurred in the northern Bitterroot Valley. Travel conditions were difficult on the morning of the 15th and again during the evening commute as bands of snow brought dramatic reductions in visibility.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lost Trail Pass is estimated to have received around 10 inches of snow in 4 hours between 7am and 11am.  Severe driving conditions were observed at Lolo Pass with 10 inches of new snow as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system centered over Wyoming, produced an inverted trough that brought heavy snow over northeast Montana as it exited east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Wibaux, 6.0 inches of snow was reported by the public.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system centered over Wyoming, produced an inverted trough that brought heavy snow over northeast Montana as it exited east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Across Prairie County, measured snowfall amounts ranged from 5.0 to 7.0 inches. The greatest amounts of 7.0 inches was reported by a CO-OP Observer at 5 N Mildred.

CZ_NAME: GALLATIN AND MADISON COUNTY MOUNTAINS AND CENTENNIAL MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of cold fronts and or upper level disturbances resulted in periods of snow early in March, primarily across Central and Southwest Montana. There were heavier bursts at times, especially around West Yellowstone and in the Helena Valley. One snow squall was observed in the Gallatin Valley the night of March 2 into March 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report was received from Big Sky Resort of 10 inches new snow by 8 AM 3/1, with 2 inch per hour rates for several hours. Additionally, several snotels, including Black Bear, Madison Plateau, Whiskey Creek, and Shower Falls estimated 9 inches of new snow or greater. The highest amount reported was 11 inches new snow at Black Bear.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved south of the area while an associated inverted trough brought areas of moderate to heavy snow across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Across western Roosevelt County, measured snowfall amounts ranged from 4.0 to 6.0 inches. The greatest amount of 6.0 inches was reported by a trained spotter in Poplar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved across northeastern Montana dropping higher amounts of snow in  Phillips County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Across northern Phillips County, measured snowfall amounts ranged from 6.9 to 8.0 inches. The greatest amounts of 8.0 inches was reported by the public at Loring.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved across northeastern Montana dropping higher amounts of snow in  Phillips County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Across central and southeast Phillips County, measured snowfall amounts ranged from 5.2 to 6.5 inches. The greatest amount of 6.5 inches was reported by the public at Saco.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough passage produced widely scattered showers and a few thunderstorms which allowed for mix-down winds between 50 and 60 mph to occur. Power outages, downed small trees and branches were reported across west-central and southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a measured wind gust at Bert Mooney airport (KBTM) in Butte of 52 knots, and 58 knots was observed at Montana Tech in Butte (MTM46).  Northwest Energy reported 552 customers without power just south of the airport. The Walmart reportedly had no power as of 4 pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late May winter storm brought several inches of snow to western MT, causing downed trees, power outages, and difficult travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Saddle Mountain SNOTEL on Lost Trail Pass recorded 10 inches of snow as of 02:00 MST May 23. A person from the public reported an estimated 3 feet of snowfall in the Sapphire Mountains, east of Florence.  There were about 1500 people without power from Painted Rocks to Sula according to Ravalli County Electric.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late May winter storm brought several inches of snow to western MT, causing downed trees, power outages, and difficult travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Utility company reports 173 without power due to heavy snow as of 00:28 MST May 23 along Interstate 90.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Glasgow Airport ASOS site (KGGW), intermittent visibility of one quarter mile or less was recorded for about 5 1/4 hours, from early to late morning on the 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper level low lifted northeast across eastern Montana on May 6th through the 9th. This low merged with a shortwave streaming from the Pacific Northwest. This storm system brought strong winds across Central and North Central Montana with the highest wind gust measured near Whitlash where a mesonet station measured an 87 mph gust. Widespread wind gusts of 55 and 75 mph were measured. Most damage was limited to trees being uprooted and power outages, but an exterior barrier of a high school roof was torn off by the wind in Shelby. Additionally, ample precipitation was observed with this system. Precipitation was mostly rain at lower elevations and fell as heavy snow in the mountains. Blizzard conditions were observed in the Little Belts, where multiple feet of snow was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MT DOT sensor at Monarch Canyon showed visibility at or below one-quarter mile for at least 7 hours (Power failed at that point). US-89 was closed from Armington Junction to White Sulphur Springs early morning May 8. The Onion Park Snotel estimated 28 inches new snow with rates of 2 inches per hour. Elsewhere, the Porphyry Peak RAWS reported sustained winds averaging 25 mph, with gusts into the 50's mph before the sensor failed at 1005 PM MDT May 7. A widespread power outage affected much of the zone through Wednesday morning. End time is estimated given lack of observations after late evening of the 7th. The Monarch Fire Department reported 48 inches storm total snow by noon Wednesday the 8th. They reported numerous trees down, trees were on houses, roadways, and in Belt Creek. Other snowfall estimates from snotels in the zone: 29 inches at Stringer Creek over 24 hours, 21 inches at Deadman Creek over 24 hours, and 16 inches of new snow at Spur Park over 24 hours. Around $114,000 in damages were done to utilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper level low lifted northeast across eastern Montana on May 6th through the 9th. This low merged with a shortwave streaming from the Pacific Northwest. This storm system brought strong winds across Central and North Central Montana with the highest wind gust measured near Whitlash where a mesonet station measured an 87 mph gust. Widespread wind gusts of 55 and 75 mph were measured. Most damage was limited to trees being uprooted and power outages, but an exterior barrier of a high school roof was torn off by the wind in Shelby. Additionally, ample precipitation was observed with this system. Precipitation was mostly rain at lower elevations and fell as heavy snow in the mountains. Blizzard conditions were observed in the Little Belts, where multiple feet of snow was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station GW1986 Lothair 9 north measured multiple gusts in excess of 58 mph, including a peak gust of 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late May winter storm brought several inches of snow to western MT, causing downed trees, power outages, and difficult travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Missoula and Ravalli Counties had about 12,500 customers without power.  A downed tree closed U.S. Highway 93 south of Conner, MT.  Although no snow was recorded in Missoula or Hamilton, three to six inches of wet, heavy snow fell throughout the Bitterroot Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late May winter storm brought several inches of snow to western MT, causing downed trees, power outages, and difficult travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Montana Department of Transportation (MDT) closed Interstate 90 over Homestake Pass east of Butte at 0530 MST May 23 due to multiple stuck vehicles.  At least two semis were jackknifed at 0500 MST.  MDT also reported 'Severe Driving Conditions' on Interstate 15 north of Butte at 2315 MST May 22.  MDT required chains to travel on US Highway 12 over MacDonald Pass west of Helena at 0138 MST May 23.  A weather spotter in Melrose reported 6 inches of snow in 3.5 hours from 0530 MST until 0900 MST. In the Porter's Corner and Georgetown Lake areas, total snowfall reports ranged from 18 to 23 inches of snowfall.

CZ_NAME: BEARS PAW MOUNTAINS AND SOUTHERN BLAINE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The latter of a few embedded shortwaves within a more broad trough across the west pivoted across the region within westerly flow aloft. Snow showers were the initial concern at higher elevation locations before a trowal pivoted southwestward toward the eastern slopes of the Rocky Mountain Front. Eventually this better forcing shifted southeastward toward the Central Montana mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 10 inches heavy wet snow in the Bears Paw. In the surrounding lower elevation areas in the forecast zone, precipitation fell entirely as rain and did not pose impacts. A Winter Weather Advisory was in effect mentioning up to 10 inches within the terrain of the Bears Paw, but nothing at lower, non-mountainous elevations within the forecast zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough passage produced widely scattered showers and a few thunderstorms which allowed for mix-down winds between 50 and 60 mph to occur. Power outages, downed small trees and branches were reported across west-central and southwest Montana.

Widespread wind gusts of 55 and 75 mph were measured. Most damage was limited to trees being uprooted and power outages, but an exterior barrier of a high school roof was torn off by the wind in Shelby. Additionally, ample precipitation was observed with this system. Precipitation was mostly rain at lower elevations and fell as heavy snow in the mountains. Blizzard conditions were observed in the Little Belts, where multiple feet of snow was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station FW9939 Chester measured multiple gusts in excess of 58 mph, including a peak of 71 mph.

CZ_NAME: RUBY MOUNTAINS AND SOUTHERN BEAVERHEAD MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex pattern developed, with a shortwave within broader troughing dropping south across BC, SE into the Pac NW and finally NE from S ID through the region. An H7 low developed across the region as the upper level forcing moved through, aiding in the development of heavy snow in the mountains. This system was cold enough for snow levels to drop to valley floors across portions of Southwest Montana. The highest snow amounts fell across SW MT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snotel equipment at JL Meadow estimated 9 inches new snow. Start, end, and verification time all estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane force winds from a rare derecho brought widespread damage to west-central Montana. The Montana governor issued an executive order declaring a disaster in both Missoula and Mineral counties due to the extent of the damage and impact.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station at the Stevensville Airport reported the gust to 66 mph originating out of the northwest. Multiple power line and pole issues were reported in the northern Ravalli County due to the winds. It appears that the Stevensville area was the furthest south extent of the derecho winds, while the Missoula area took a direct hit.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Frenchtown Rural Fire District rpeorted a structure fire after a pine tree crashed onto a power line in Alberton, causing the lines to arc. The Mineral County Disaster and Emergency Services reported that its phone lines were down. The severe winds of the derecho clipped the southeastern third of Mineral County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane force winds from a rare derecho brought widespread damage to west-central Montana. The Montana governor issued an executive order declaring a disaster in both Missoula and Mineral counties due to the extent of the damage and impact.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A state of emergency was declared by both the Missoula County Commission Chair and Missoula Mayor because of the unprecedented wind damage that occurred. The 80 to 100 mph winds brought thousands of trees down across the county. It wasn't just a quick gust, but rather high winds that lasted for a couple of minutes. This led to power and transmission poles breaking in half and triggering power outages that lasted for up to a week after, affecting close to 40,000 customers, many of which had to toss out spoiled food. One utility reported 80 power poles were broken across their service area with 42 of those knocked down or shattered along Mullan Road, located approximately 7 miles west of US-93. Many traffic lights did not work until two days after the event. The Missoula County 911 call center received over 600 calls with many people not getting through. The main repeater on Horseback Ridge, located southwest of Missoula, went down because of the wind and firefighters could only use line-of-sight radio communication throughout the night. Natural gas meters and natural gas lines were damaged. The DNRC gave a conservative estimate that 1,000 city trees were uprooted, splintered at the truck or suffered some other total failure. Their Missoula Campus lost 52 mature ponderosa pines, and over 25 tree species. Water conservation restrictions were put up in place because the Missoula Water was running on generators. Many parts of the city were shut down including parks and mainstem rivers due to downed power lines. Live power lines trapped many people traveling through the city, including two teenagers who sat in their car for over an hour until rescue personnel could reach them in the Lower Miller Creek area. Over a hundred homes had some sort of shingle or siding damage, and many could not get roofers to fix it until 2 months later. Portions of the Blackfoot River were closed due to downed power lines and damaged power poles and lines were also reported in the Clinton, Salmon Lake, Condon and Seeley Lake areas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: On July 21, Libby saw a high of 104 degrees, while the Libby Dam COOP reported 103 degrees, and Eureka experienced a reading of 100 degrees. The Troy area saw highs in the low 100s for three days straight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Relatively deep moisture associated with a mid level disturbance shifted across the region, resulting in periods of moderate to heavy snowfall. Snow amounts varied quite a bit, with three main areas of heavier snow: Along the Hi-Line, SE Cascade county, and the Helena Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 7 inches new snow in Belt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad trough pushed across the Intermountain West, as a storm system rode up along the trough into northeast Montana.  The precipitation began as a mix due to existing warmer than normal air over the region, before eventually changing over to snow as the event progressed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Across Petroleum County, measured snowfall amounts ranged from 2.0 to 6.5 inches. The greatest amount of 6.5 inches was reported by a trained spotter 14S Winnett.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a period of record cold temperatures, a moist Pacific system produced an overrunning snow event across most of the Billings Forecast Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 12+ inches were recorded in the mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cool northwesterly flow aloft set up across the region early July, allowing for numerous showers and thunderstorms. Any severe reports early in the month will be in this episode.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northwesterly flow aloft combined with ample instability and shear to result in a few supercells across Hill and Blaine counties. Hail was the main severe caliber threat, though there was a wind report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the first of two heatwaves that western Montana experienced in July of 2024.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The high of 97 at Kalispell on the 11th broke the previous daily record of 95 set back in 1975. The SKQ Dam also reported a record high of 98 on the 11th which broke the previous record of 97 degrees set back in 1953. The hottest temperature reported was 103 degrees in Hot Springs on the 10th.

Between 630-830 PM MDT, severe thunderstorm winds moved across Custer County and over the town of Miles City. Wind speeds up to 77 mph were reported resulting in widespread significant tree damage including the uprooting of large trees which in some cases fell on vehicles and other property. Downed power lines caused power outages across the Miles City area. At least one rooftop was reported blown off a house. Power outages and large tree damage was also reported in Fallon County in Baker where the airport recorded a wind gust of 79 mph.  In addition, one location in northwest Custer County recorded 2.92 inches of rainfall in 30 minutes and hail up to nickel size covered the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree and powerline damage, along with power outages, were reported throughout Miles City.

CZ_NAME: BIG BELT, BRIDGER AND CASTLE MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long stretch of hot and dry conditions transitioned much of the Northern Rockies into fire weather season by mid-July. This episode contains notable fires in the CWA that began early-mid July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Horse Gulch fire developed the afternoon of July 9 and quickly spread eastward on the 10th and 11th due to windy conditions. As of July 26, the fire was declared 100% contained.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave within southwesterly flow aloft resulted in a few thunderstorms June 20 ahead of a more robust shortwave that brought numerous strong to severe thunderstorms to the region June 21. Moist low level easterly flow, the passing shortwave and ample westerly flow aloft resulted in a favorable environment for supercells the 21st, primarily capable of producing large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 1 to 2 inch hail with torrential rainfall. The hail completely covered the ground, making it white.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific front kicked off a period of strong winds across Central and North-central Montana late day June 3. The initial period of stronger winds was attributed to mountain wave activity along and adjacent to the Rocky Mountain Front. A second surge of robust westerly flow just off the surface developed Tuesday the 4th, lasting into the overnight. Daytime mixing helped with translating gusts to the surface during the day on the 4th, with mountain waves taking over as the mechanism by nighttime. Winds subsided by the morning of the 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: RAWS station JPKM8 Judith Peak measured multiple gusts in excess of 58 mph, including a peak gust of 73 mph.

A second surge of robust westerly flow just off the surface developed Tuesday the 4th, lasting into the overnight. Daytime mixing helped with translating gusts to the surface during the day on the 4th, with mountain waves taking over as the mechanism by nighttime. Winds subsided by the morning of the 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station MTBRN Two Medicine Bridge measured two gusts in excess of 58 mph, including a peak gust of 71 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwesterly flow aloft allowed for showers and thunderstorms to develop ahead of a quickly advancing cold front June 15 across Central and North-Central Montana. Not many severe-caliber gusts were observed. Additionally, deep mixing across Southwest Montana ahead of the front allowed for a few strong wind gusts there June 15. Along and behind the front, briefly strong non-convective wind gusts were observed along and adjacent to the Rocky Mountain Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS station KDLN Dillon Airport measured a 58 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave within southwesterly flow aloft resulted in a few thunderstorms June 20 ahead of a more robust shortwave that brought numerous strong to severe thunderstorms to the region June 21. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Time and location estimated. Hail stones between 1 and 1.5 inches were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave within strong westerly flow aloft helped translate isolated strong winds and gusts to the surface along the Rocky Mountain Front and adjacent plains during the late evening of 4/8.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted longwave trough with an associated closed low lifted NE from ID toward the northern US. Gulf moisture was drawn northwestward across eastern portions of the plains thanks to a developing surface low across the Great Plains. Isentropic ascent and favorable upper level dynamics related in a band of snow, impacting Fergus county the greatest. mid level northwesterly flow resulted in a single wind gust in excess of 58 mph in eastern Fergus county, where snow was less impactful.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOTEL equipment at Crystal Lake estimated 14 inches new snow over the 10 hours prior to verification time. The SNOTEL estimated 18 inches storm total snow. Additionally, a Co-Op observer measured 13.5 inches new snow over 24 hours ending 7 AM MDT April 7.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted longwave trough with an associated closed low lifted NE from ID toward the northern US. Gulf moisture was drawn northwestward across eastern portions of the plains thanks to a developing surface low across the Great Plains. Isentropic ascent and favorable upper level dynamics related in a band of snow, impacting Fergus county the greatest. mid level northwesterly flow resulted in a single wind gust in excess of 58 mph in eastern Fergus county, where snow was less impactful.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snotel equipment at Spur Park measured 10 inches storm total snow over 24 hours. Additionally, Showdown Ski area measured 10 inches new snow over 24 hours. Given warm pavement temperatures, less intense snow than across Fergus County, and the limited spatial extent of snow reaching winter storm criteria snow amounts, Winter Storm caliber impacts were not realized over a noteworthy portion of this forecast zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak convection the morning and afternoon of April 5 translated two gusts in excess of 58 mph to the surface across Southwest Montana. The overall convective environment was weak from a thermodynamic perspective, but strong southerly winds ahead of an approaching trough made up for the thermodynamic shortcomings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak shower moved over Bozeman overnight on the 5th, which resulted in a heat burst in town. A personal weather device on Sourdough Ridge Rd (Ambient Wx station) measured a gust in excess of 60 mph, with temperatures nearby rising by nearly 10 degrees over 10 minutes overnight. Broadcast media reported tree damage in Bozeman from the heat burst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted longwave trough with an associated closed low lifted NE from ID toward the northern US. Gulf moisture was drawn northwestward across eastern portions of the plains thanks to a developing surface low across the Great Plains. Isentropic ascent and favorable upper level dynamics related in a band of snow, impacting Fergus county the greatest. mid level northwesterly flow resulted in a single wind gust in excess of 58 mph in eastern Fergus county, where snow was less impactful.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS station K1OM Oscar measured a 59 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper low over the Great Basin and vicinity shifted northeastward toward the region while it spawned a surface low across eastern MT. As the upper level disturbance and surface low became stacked across eastern MT, a period of widespread heavy rain developed where the TROWAL set up across the eastern plains. Additionally, a few strong northwesterly gusts were observed on the west side of the surface low. Overall, the soil was quite dry before the onset of the event, which allowed for heavy rain to be largely beneficial.||Rain amounts from this event were greatest across the eastern plains (Fergus, Blaine, Hill, Chouteau, and Judith Basin counties), largely ranging between 2 and 5 inches, with a few reports of upwards of 7 and a half inches. Areas that reported rain in excess of 6 inches include 10 S of Chinook in Blaine county (7.42), 5 NE Hilger in Fergus county (7.25), 2 S Chinook in Blaine county (6.6), and 7 SE Lewistown in Fergus county (6.5). ASOS station KLWT Lewistown Airport measured 4.35 over 24 hours during the calendar day of September 18 (Standard time), which was the second highest single day precipitation total on record in the Lewistown climate records.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: RAWS station JPKM8 Judith Peak measured two gusts in excess of 60 mph, including a peak gust of 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust shortwave shifting eastward from the Pacific Northwest eclipsed the Northern Rockies during the day October 4. At the surface, a surface trough slowly increased in magnitude through the day before ejecting eastward as a surface low late afternoon. As the surface low pulled away, a strong cold front (Aided by 50-70kt winds within the first few thousand feet of the surface) swept across the region. Mechanical mixing allowed for multiple 70+ mph gusts to be realized at the surface, even with the front passing after sunset for most locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station BFTM 4.4 miles east of Hogeland (Agrimet) measured a 58 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced 50 to 60 mph winds in select valleys of northwest Montana. The damaging wind caused tree damage, and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A utility company reported over 500 people without power northwest of Thompson Falls to the Noxon area. The Montana Department of Transportation reported a tree that blocked U.S.-2 and S-482, located just south of Libby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced 50 to 60 mph winds in select valleys of northwest Montana. The damaging wind caused tree damage, and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Glacier National Park reported multiple trees down including 12 trees along the North Fork Road, trees down near the Walton Ranger Station near Essex and mile-marker 182 on U.S. Highway 2, trees down along Camas Road, near Lake MacDonald and in Apgar.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN GLACIER, WESTERN TOOLE, AND CENTRAL PONDERA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A longwave trough and attendant closed upper low diving southeastward from the Gulf of Alaska brought increasing zonal mid level flow across the Northern Rockies. An approaching shortwave within the broader trough as well as cold air advection allowed for strong winds and gusts to translate to the surface during the day October 27.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS station KCTB measured multiple gusts in excess of 58 mph, including a peak gust of 64 mph. The ASOS also measured sustained winds, averaged over an hour, of 42 mph between 1230 and 130 PM MDT. Additionally, mesonet sensors MTSWT Sweet Grass (MT DOT) and MTM05 ARC-WTri Conrad also measured gusts of 58 mph or greater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wildfires that carried into October will be documented here.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sheep Creek fire was a lightning start (September 17) about 5 miles SW of Bernice, MT in the Boulder Mountains. The size of the fire is 778 acres as of writing, with 54% of operational objectives complete on the fire as of November 7, 2024. Fuels involved include live trees, dead lodgepole pines with a layer of grass and litter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced 50 to 60 mph winds in select valleys of northwest Montana. The damaging wind caused tree damage, and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least three trees fell due to estimated 60 mph winds in Moiese. The largest tree fell onto a house and cause considerable damage. Over two thousand customers were without power during the event. A large pine tree fell onto a yard located north of Polson. The Glacier Park International airport ASOS recorded a peak gust to 59 mph at 1719 PM MDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced 50 to 60 mph winds in select valleys of northwest Montana. The damaging wind caused tree damage, and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several private weather stations between Lolo and Victor, on the west side of US-93 reported wind gusts between 66 and 83 mph. The Stevensville RAWS recorded a peak wind gust to 64 mph. The winds were strong enough to push over both a porta john and watch for wildlife road sign. Several mature ponderosa pine trees were uprooted west of Bell Crossing. Close to one thousand people were without power due to trees on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced 50 to 60 mph winds in select valleys of northwest Montana. The damaging wind caused tree damage, and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Frequent wind gusts between 50 and 60 mph were recorded by the Deer Mountain, Tepee Point and Point 6 RAWS stations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures and dry conditions during October allowed drought conditions to continue.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To start October, ongoing D-2 Severe drought conditions continued over the northeastern third of Richland County. On the second week of October, the northeastern third of Richland County worsened to D-3 Extreme drought, and the central strip of the county worsened to D-2 Severe drought. On the third week of October the western half of Richland County worsened to D-2 Severe drought, the middle strip worsened to D-3 Extreme drought, making the eastern half of the county all in D-3 Extreme drought.  These conditions remained the same through the end of October.

On the third week of October, the western quarter of Eastern Roosevelt County had worsened to D-2 Severe drought, with the eastern three quarters of the county now all in D-3 Extreme drought. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderately strong cross barrier flow and a developing pressure gradient allowed for gusts in excess of 75 mph along the Rocky Mountain Front late October 1 for about an hour or two.

CZ_NAME: GALLATIN AND MADISON COUNTY MOUNTAINS AND CENTENNIAL MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A splitting trough resulted in a period of heavy snow across the high terrain of Madison and Gallatin counties late Oct 17 into the 18th. Further, as the trough pulled away, a secondary wave diving SE from BC resulted in strong cross barrier flow, ridgetop stability and hence strong wind gusts across the plains adjacent to the Rocky Mountain Front. A strong pressure gradient also helped stronger gusts occur near the Rocky Mountain Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snotel equipment at Shower Falls estimated 9 inches new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With October starting off dry, drought conditions expanded westward into Dawson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the second week of October, D2-Severe drought expanded into the far eastern border of Dawson County.  On the third week of October, D2-Severe drought spread into the northern half of Dawson County and pushed a little more east on the southeastern portion of the county, while D3-Extreme drought moved into the eastern border of Dawson county along the Wibaux County border.  On the fourth week of October, all but the far eastern border of Dawson County had worsened to D-2 Severe drought, with the easter border of the county along the Wibaux County border remaining in D3-Extreme drought.  These conditions remained the same through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wildfires that carried into October will be documented here.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pedro Mine Fire was a lightning start fire (September 4th) in the southern Tobacco Root Mountains, 9 miles east of Sheridan. The fire grew to 150 acres before operational objectives were complete on the fire on October 24. The fire burned in steep, rocky terrain with little to no access to fire fighters. Lots of snags in the area led to the decision to allow the fire to burn in most areas, but protect critical assets that were nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between 430pm and 530pm, a severe thunderstorm hit the Billings area, producing wind gusts up to 78 mph and causing extensive damage. The storm came from the northwest, and intensified quickly in the hour leading up to its arrival in Billings. After gusting to 76 mph at the Billings Airport, the thunderstorm went on from Billings and continued to produce gusts 70-80 mph through Crow Agency and Lame Deer before quickly weakening as it went further east. The storm fortunately missed Hardin by just a few miles, passing by just to the south of town. ||Immense damage was reported across the Billings area, and likely continued to occur with the storm as it traveled through Crow Agency. Multiple power lines were downed, and many large trees were snapped or completely uprooted. A combination of both caused power outages across Billings and an assortment of damages to homes and businesses. In addition, the brief, torrential rain produced localized flash flooding across town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level wave brought a cold front and associated stronger winds aloft to the region the evening of the 27th through the early morning on the 29th. After the front, a pressure gradient developed along the Rocky Mountain Front that helped keep stronger winds going into the 28th, which prompted High Wind Warnings. A few rouge strong wind gusts were observed across the plains east of the warning into the 29th, but were too few and far in between to warrant widespread High Wind Warnings. Additionally, snow level dropped just enough to result in a period of heavy snow at Logan Pass behind the front on the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Glacier NP officials reported between 10 and 12 inches of snow at 6650 elevation near Logan Pass. People and vehicles were stranded, with slide offs reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The main day for severe weather was August 6th. A rather robust shortwave progressing across the region combined with ample surface heating to result in a round of severe thunderstorms across the region. Outflow from the previous days convection inhibited thunderstorms across most of the plains through the day, but thunderstorms forming off the high terrain of Glacier county spread southeastward with time and resulted in some damaging wind. Another rogue strong wind gust was observed on the 10th associated with a deep inverted-V sounding in Dillon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 1 inch diameter hail.

A few rouge strong wind gusts were observed across the plains east of the warning into the 29th, but were too few and far in between to warrant widespread High Wind Warnings. Additionally, snow level dropped just enough to result in a period of heavy snow at Logan Pass behind the front on the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS station K1BM Charlie measured a 58 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The main day for severe weather was August 6th. A rather robust shortwave progressing across the region combined with ample surface heating to result in a round of severe thunderstorms across the region. Outflow from the previous days convection inhibited thunderstorms across most of the plains through the day, but thunderstorms forming off the high terrain of Glacier county spread southeastward with time and resulted in some damaging wind. Another rogue strong wind gust was observed on the 10th associated with a deep inverted-V sounding in Dillon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported heavy rain and quarter sized hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported several instances of damage on their farm, mainly due to strong wind gusts. The report included mention of roof and siding damage, in addition to barn door damage, a blown over auger, and a blown over 250 gallon container. Visibility was around 10 ft at the peak of the event and 0.80 inches of rain accumulated over 4 minutes. A follow up report from the same source mentioned upon further inspection, the combination of strong wind gusts and hail resulted in damage to crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell formed along the Canadian border in the afternoon and moved across far northeastern Montana.  Later at night a large supercell formed in Garfield County and moved across the Dry Arm of Fort Peck Lake causing extensive damage to Rock Creek Marina.  This supercell eventually evolved into a line of storms and moved across northeast Montana on the morning of the 25th before weakening and exiting the area before sunrise.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An emergency manager reported a cabin at Rock Creek Marina, on Fort Peck Lake, had its roof blown off.  Thunderstorm wind speeds were estimated to be between 70 and 80 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An emergency manager, on Fort Peck Lake, reported a garage door blown in, a camper had been flipped over, an antenna on a hill had blown over and appeared twisted, a courtesy dock at the boat ramp flipped over, and a trailered pontoon boat was knocked over.  Wind gusts were estimated between 70 and 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong upper level trough was set up just off the west coast, which resulted in a strong belt of mid level southerly flow across Southwest through North-central Montana. Synoptic winds were already breezy from the south, allowing thunderstorms to easily produce strong wind gusts given inverted-V soundings. Wind gusts in excess of 80 mph were observed on this day as a line of gusty outflow spread northward over the plains.||A High Wind Warning was also issued for Southwest Montana this day, though no strong synoptic gusts were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ambient Weather Sensor measured gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active week of severe weather unfolded across the Northern Rockies from August 18th through the 21st. Subtle waves lifting through a southwesterly flow aloft just west of an upper level ridge combined with ample surface heating and shear to result in multiple rounds of severe weather, most notably on August 19th when a line of strong thunderstorms lifted northward across the plains and adjacent areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports quarter sized hail. Gusty winds were also observed, which blew around items at the residence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between 430pm and 530pm, a severe thunderstorm hit the Billings area, producing wind gusts up to 78 mph and causing extensive damage. The storm came from the northwest, and intensified quickly in the hour leading up to its arrival in Billings. After gusting to 76 mph at the Billings Airport, the thunderstorm went on from Billings and continued to produce gusts 70-80 mph through Crow Agency and Lame Deer before quickly weakening as it went further east. The storm fortunately missed Hardin by just a few miles, passing by just to the south of town. ||Immense damage was reported across the Billings area, and likely continued to occur with the storm as it traveled through Crow Agency. Multiple power lines were downed, and many large trees were snapped or completely uprooted. A combination of both caused power outages across Billings and an assortment of damages to homes and businesses. In addition, the brief, torrential rain produced localized flash flooding across town.

The Remington Fire began in Sheridan County Wyoming and quickly spread north into Montana as a result of a low-level nocturnal jet. Total acreage burned is estimated at 196,368 acres. As of this report, the cause of the wildfire remains unknown. Total agricultural and structural damage is also unknown. Extensive air attacks were used by the firefighters during the lead-up to the formation of the low level nocturnal jet. In addition, the Northern Cheyenne Tribal Council issued a mandatory evacuation for the town of Birney. ||For more detailed information, please see inciweb.wildfire.gov/incident-information/mtgnf-remington-fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active week of severe weather unfolded across the Northern Rockies from August 18th through the 21st. Subtle waves lifting through a southwesterly flow aloft just west of an upper level ridge combined with ample surface heating and shear to result in multiple rounds of severe weather, most notably on August 19th when a line of strong thunderstorms lifted northward across the plains and adjacent areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS station KHVR Haver Airport measured a severe caliber gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between 430pm and 530pm, a severe thunderstorm hit the Billings area, producing wind gusts up to 78 mph and causing extensive damage. The storm came from the northwest, and intensified quickly in the hour leading up to its arrival in Billings. After gusting to 76 mph at the Billings Airport, the thunderstorm went on from Billings and continued to produce gusts 70-80 mph through Crow Agency and Lame Deer before quickly weakening as it went further east. The storm fortunately missed Hardin by just a few miles, passing by just to the south of town. ||Immense damage was reported across the Billings area, and likely continued to occur with the storm as it traveled through Crow Agency. Multiple power lines were downed, and many large trees were snapped or completely uprooted. A combination of both caused power outages across Billings and an assortment of damages to homes and businesses. In addition, the brief, torrential rain produced localized flash flooding across town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power pole was snapped and hanging.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong upper level trough was set up just off the west coast, which resulted in a strong belt of mid level southerly flow across Southwest through North-central Montana. Synoptic winds were already breezy from the south, allowing thunderstorms to easily produce strong wind gusts given inverted-V soundings. Wind gusts in excess of 80 mph were observed on this day as a line of gusty outflow spread northward over the plains.||A High Wind Warning was also issued for Southwest Montana this day, though no strong synoptic gusts were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The time and location was estimated. A farmer stated that freshly cut hay was blown by approximately 80 mph winds.

An early season snowfall event brough about 10 to 12 inches of snow to Glacier National Park.  Park officials closed roads, but people were still stranded in campgrounds and buses had slid off the roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Glacier Park rangers reported 10 to 12 inches of snow.  The Logan Pass snow measuring site recorded 11 inches in 24 hours.  The Park closed the Going to the Sun Road.  The storm stranded park visitors, and buses slid off the road.  One person was killed in a slip and fall accident on Sperry Trail on August 28.  Media sources identify the deceased as a 65 year old male who slipped and hit his head.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This episode will document notable wildfires in the area that occurred at some point in August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: USFS reported the Black Mountain Fire, 6 miles NW of Lincoln, was first reported July 29 in the afternoon. The fire grew to 182 acres before becoming fully contained August 21. The cause was undetermined and fuels involved were closed timber litter and brush on the east side of Black Mountain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Horse Gulch Fire began July 9th about a half mile north of Canyon Ferry Dam near Helena. The fire was human caused and grew to 15,167 acres before becoming 100% contained on August 14. Fuels involved included timber, brush and cured grasses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm air advection aloft in advance of an approaching Pacific trough allowed for mainly light, but localized heavy snow to develop across the region December 29th into the 30th. A band of snow developed across Southwest Montana early in the event and progressed northward, but never stalled anywhere for too long, thus only resulting in localized heavy snow reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snotel equipment at Black Bear estimated 10 inches new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another atmospheric river brought high winds and a wintry mix to western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous power outages were reported from the south side of Flathead Lake to the Mission Valley. Frequent wind gusts over 50 miles per hour and even gusts over 60 miles per hour were reported with a peak gust of 67 miles per hour in Ronan. At least 2000 to 3000 customers were without power in the Mission Valley from multiple outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another atmospheric river brought high winds and a wintry mix to western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation started as snow then turned to rain and eventually back to snow with slick roadways and multiple accidents on Highway 200 and Interstate 90. Snow amounts were light, generally less than two inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak disturbances in northwest flow brought widespread light to moderate snow with localized heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell over the Flathead Valley but the far eastern part of the Valley along the foothills of the Swan Range received locally heavy snow.  Areas near Lake Blaine and Echo Lake picked up 8 to 14 inches of new snow. An interesting note, Noisy Basin SNOTEL had 4.6 inches of precipitation over 48 hours, making it the second highest 48 hour total on record for that station during the winter season.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another atmospheric river brought high winds and a wintry mix to western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain on snow caused icy conditions. Winds gusted up to 55 mph near Marias Pass and up to 82 mph on the higher mountain ridges. Localized power outages were observed near Swan Lake with 171 customers losing power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another atmospheric river brought high winds and a wintry mix to western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak gusts of 66 miles per hour at MacDonald Pass, 62 mph at Warm Springs Pond and 55 mph at Elliston were observed during the wind event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance aloft moved across far east and southeast Montana during the early morning and afternoon hours of the 15th bringing scattered rain showers across the area due to the mild temperatures. However, surface temperatures were at or slightly below freezing. This resulted in a period of freezing rain during the early morning hours lasting into the mid to later morning hours until surface temperatures warmed above freezing. |The Miles City Airport reported 0.08 inches of ice at 3:06am. In addition, freezing rain and black ice were reported on roadways. Interstate I-94 from Miles City to Glendive, along with US 212 from Alzada to the Wyoming border were closed for a period of time. Multiple vehicle crashes were reported throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Please see Episode narrative.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge across the Northern Rockies was flattened by a potent shortwave over Dec 6-8 timeframe. Initially stout zonal mid-level flow was aided by a strengthening pressure gradient to result in quite a few strong winds gusts over the multi-day period. Adequate moistures resulted in locally heavy snow along the Rocky Mountain Front and into Central Montana by Dec 9.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station MTM48 Chinook E - MDA measured a 59 mph gust.

CZ_NAME: BIG BELT, BRIDGER AND CASTLE MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust trough digging southeastward from the Pacific Northwest brought a belt of strong mid and low-level westerly flow across the majority of the region December 18. Given the favorable timing of the strongest flow and cold front, winds were easily able to mix to the surface. A respectable pressure gradient also developed across the area, which helped a few sites reach high sustained winds. In northeastern areas the wind took longer to develop, eventually needing the surface low to pull away from AB toward SK. This propelled a front through the area that brought both strong winds as well as rain that fell onto very cold surfaces, resulting in slick roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet sensor Bridger Canoyon (MT DOT) measured multiple gusts of 58 mph or greater, including a peak gust of 72 mph. Additional sensors in this zone to measure gusts of 58 mph or greater include RAWS station TT838 Battle Ridge, Weather Underground site Pass CK BAPH - KMTBELGR75, Weather Underground site Big Belt - KMTWHITE67, and mesonet station MTDPC Deep Creek Us12 Mp 23.5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak disturbances in northwest flow brought widespread light to moderate snow with localized heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain in the early morning hours resulting in icy conditions on Highway 2 between Libby and Happys Inn. Precipitation turned to snow with 6 to 8 inches near Crystal Creek during the daylight hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river brought freezing rain as valleys remained under inversions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to a quarter inch of ice accretion for many valleys of Lincoln County, including the towns of Libby and Troy. Untreated roads and sidewalks were very icy with many slide offs reported. Side roads were the worst but even primary highways had periods of ice cover. There were reports that some people avoided travel because of the conditions. There was also a power outage in Libby that was related to the weather event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain on snow along with gusty winds to 40 mph caused power outages from Olney to Stryker with isolated outages along Therriault Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong cross barrier flow combined with a tightening pressure gradient allowed for a period of gusty winds along the Rocky Mountain Front Dec 26 into Dec 27. CZ_NAME: GALLATIN AND MADISON COUNTY MOUNTAINS AND CENTENNIAL MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong mid level winds associated with a passing shortwave combined with two cold fronts (One Pacific and one Canadian), to result in two separate periods of strong winds, initially along the Rocky Mountain Front, but over portions of the plains as well later in the event. Behind the second Canadian cold front, northwesterly upslope snow developed across island ranges and in the Little Belts, which resulted in the greatest snow total for the event. Locations on the plains between Belt and Stanford also observed heavy snow. Additional heavy snow was observed further south across the Madison and Gallatin Ranges as westerly and eventually northwesterly flow allowed for a steady, but accumulating snow. A Blizzard Warning was also issued for the Rocky Mountain Front during portions of this period, but the period of strongest winds did not end up coinciding with falling snow, hence these potential impacts were never observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Big Sky Ski resort reported 11 inches new snow between the evening of November 4 and early morning November 6. Start, end and criteria time were all estimated due to poor radar coverage and limited report details. Additionally, severe driving conditions were reported in the West Yellowstone area by MT DOT late evening November 4th, but no further details were received.

Behind the second Canadian cold front, northwesterly upslope snow developed across island ranges and in the Little Belts, which resulted in the greatest snow total for the event. Locations on the plains between Belt and Stanford also observed heavy snow. Additional heavy snow was observed further south across the Madison and Gallatin Ranges as westerly and eventually northwesterly flow allowed for a steady, but accumulating snow. A Blizzard Warning was also issued for the Rocky Mountain Front during portions of this period, but the period of strongest winds did not end up coinciding with falling snow, hence these potential impacts were never observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montana mesonet station acemtleb Mt Lebanon SE measured a 61 mph gust east of the Sweet Grass Hills. Additionally, mesonet station GW3546 and GW0718 measured 61 mph and 60 mph gusts respectively in northern Liberty county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although the region was between systems, strong cross barrier flow along the Rocky Mountain Front and a modest ridgetop inversion resulted in mountain waves over the adjacent plains. Additionally, strong winds below the inversion in and around Great Falls were translated to the surface over multiple hours the morning of the 17th, with gusts in excess of 70 mph observed at the Great Falls Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montana Mesonet site DOGGU Dog Gun Lake E measured a 76 mph gust. Additionally, mesonet sensor MTBWG Browning DOT (72 mph), mesonet sensor MTBRN Two Medicine Bridge DOT (61 mph), and mesonet station SHBM Browning (65 mph) all measured criteria gusts.

CZ_NAME: GALLATIN AND MADISON COUNTY MOUNTAINS AND CENTENNIAL MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This episode will contain wildfires that carried over into November. By the end of November, all fires had operational objectives completed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Meridian fire was an undetermined start wildfire that started 40 miles south of Ennis. The primary fuel involved with the fire was timber with a grass and litter understory. The fire grew to 3,900 acres before operational objectives were declared 100% complete in early November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient developed across Southwest Montana on November 13. Strong surface high pressure in the Yellowstone NP vicinity combined with relatively low pressure over the plains to result in multiple gusts of 58 mph and multiple instances of sustained high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS station KEKS Ennis Airport measured a 61 mph gust as well as sustained winds over an hour averaging 41 mph. Additionally, mesonet sensor MTNOR Norris Hill (MT DOT) measured high sustained winds (Averaged 40 mph over 3 hrs 45 mins) and a gust of 59 mph.

CZ_NAME: BEARS PAW MOUNTAINS AND SOUTHERN BLAINE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick clipper like system dove southeastward through a northwesterly flow aloft the evening of Nov 27 into the 28th. A narrow band of enhanced snowfall rates developed in Judith Basin county, perhaps aided by convergence around terrain, to result in 6 inches new snow in the Stanford area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer in Stanford measured 6 inches new snow over 24 hours. Radar was used to estimate a more specific time range for the snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This episode will contain wildfires that carried over into November. By the end of November, all fires had operational objectives completed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sheep Creek Fire was a lightning start 5 miles southwest of Bernice on September 22. Fuels involved were timber with a layer of grass and litter. The fire grew to 778 acres as of the last update. Although operational objectives are only listed as 54% complete, the incident is no longer being updated. Hence this will be the last update for the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A category 4 atmospheric river hit northern California and southwestern Oregon. The remnant moisture brought 1.50 to 3 inches of precipitation to the mountains of the Northern Rockies. Modified arctic air moved into northern Lincoln County, Flathead Valley and in the Glacier National Park region Wednesday night (Nov 20) onwards and allowed for snow to fall in Yaak and Eureka, and also Essex to Marias Pass. Heavy wet snow caused extensive tree damage and power outages.  Precipitation periodically changed to freezing rain, adding to the tree damage and causing treacherous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montana Department of Transportation reported about a foot of snow at Java (east of Essex). MDT stated there has been blowing and drifting east of Java, and described the situation as a nightmare for them to keep up the roads the last 36 hours. They were fighting with ice, especially when people pull off in the wet dense snow. This was mainly a problem between West Glacier to Essex, where it has been warmer. They  said the worst timeframe for them was November 23 between 4am and 10am MST when precipitation was transitioning from rain to snow.  Social media conveyed several reports of motor vehicle accidents, downed trees, and power outages.  Flathead Electric   reported several thousand without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A category 4 atmospheric river hit northern California and southwestern Oregon. The remnant moisture brought 1.50 to 3 inches of precipitation to the mountains of the Northern Rockies. Modified arctic air moved into northern Lincoln County, Flathead Valley and in the Glacier National Park region Wednesday night (Nov 20) onwards and allowed for snow to fall in Yaak and Eureka, and also Essex to Marias Pass. Heavy wet snow caused extensive tree damage and power outages.  Precipitation periodically changed to freezing rain, adding to the tree damage and causing treacherous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple reports of heavy, wet snow and freezing rain causing motor vehicle accidents, broken tree limbs, and downed power lines.  The snow primarily fell from Yaak to Olney in this zone, 8-10 inches as of the morning of the 23rd.  Rain resulted in Boorman Creek to breach its banks (no damage from flooding was reported).  A major power outage was reported in Lincoln County lasting 24 to 36 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm rapidly developed in Saskatchewan, turned right and tracked southeastward into Montana.
